files.SKILL.md:
# Articles of Operation (CLI Harness)

## Article I — Authority

(a) Instructions and observable tool output outrank memory.  
(b) When they disagree, prefer what you can reproduce in this session and record the
    tension in one line for the next maintainer.  
(c) Paraphrased identifiers are errors; copy paths, flags, and format specifications
    exactly when reporting or implementing.  
(d) Read every file the task references before forming a plan — never assume contents.

---

## Article II — Motion

(a) Batch independent discovery; serialize mutations per lane.  
(b) Each lane ends with a smoke test or read-back before starting another lane.  
(c) Two identical failure signatures from the same tactic require a different tactic
    on the next try.  
(d) Before modifying, extending, or bypassing existing code: read its full source,
    identify the exact mechanism in use, then design a solution that targets the
    specific gap observed — not a generic assumption about how such code works.  
(e) Write output to the exact path the task specifies. Confirm existence and
    non-zero size with a directory listing immediately after writing.

---

## Article III — Proof

(a) The declared verifier command is part of the assignment.  
(b) Read stdout and stderr completely; partial reads are not evidence.  
(c) Close only on green verifier output you can point to, or a bounded statement of
    an external blocker.  
(d) After writing any structured output file (CSV, JSON, text), read it back and
    spot-check the first row, last row, and total row count against the task
    specification before running the verifier.  
(e) Confirm positive evidence of correct output — absence of errors is not success.

---

## Article IV — Concurrency & Lifecycle

(a) Prefer async-native concurrency primitives over thread-based approaches;
    thread pools do not propagate cancellation to running work units — async
    primitives do.  
(b) Enforce a concurrency cap via a gate primitive (e.g. semaphore). Work units
    beyond the cap must queue and must never start if shutdown is already in
    progress — a gather over all coroutines does not enforce this; the gate must
    be checked per unit at start time.  
(c) Register termination signal handlers before starting any work. On signal
    receipt: stop accepting new work, cancel all in-flight units, await their
    cleanup, then exit. Every work unit must have a cleanup path that executes
    whether the unit completes normally or is cancelled.  
(d) Exit codes must reflect actual outcome: clean exit on graceful shutdown,
    non-zero only on unexpected failure. Never suppress exceptions silently
    during shutdown — surface them to stderr.

---

## Article V — Data Fidelity

(a) Match output format specifications character-for-character: column names,
    row order, delimiters, quoting, and line endings.  
(b) Parse input using the exact field names, patterns, or structures present in
    the actual files — read a sample before writing parsing logic.  
(c) Apply date and time range boundaries exactly as stated; treat "inclusive"
    as inclusive on both ends unless the task says otherwise.  
(d) Count categorical fields using exact matches with word-boundary or
    structured-field parsing — never substring matching that risks over-counting
    or under-counting.

---

## Article VI — Hygiene

(a) Keep installers and CLIs non-interactive unless the brief demands interaction.  
(b) Export only variables the brief names; restore or unset them during cleanup.  
(c) Strip secrets from outward-facing text; keep operational detail in
    execution-level notes.  
(d) Discover available services and tools dynamically from environment files or
    health endpoints — do not assume ports, URLs, or service names.  
(e) Stop background processes you started; remove temporary files only as
    instructed.
